*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.

::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility Is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. In general, people in Connecticut serving a definite sentence of more than two years where the underlying offense does not involve the use or threat of physical force become eligible for parole after serving one-half of the maximum sentence length. The parole board determines the applicability of the use of force criterion, which requires a person to serve 85 percent of the maximum to be eligible for parole release.¹
## In Prison Past Parole Eligibility In 2019, 35 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 65 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` From 2010 to 2020, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 18.3 percent. We've projected that from 2021 to 2023, the percent of people past parole eligibility will decrease by 0.7 percent. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity In 2019, 43 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were Black, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex In 2019, 96 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age In 2019, 33 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types In 2019, 39 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 61 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people in prison past parole eligibility were incarcerated for public order (26 percent) offenses. ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths In 2019, 37 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 2 and 4.9 years. ```{=html}
```
## Estimation Methodology Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state's parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.

To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (85 percent for those who's most serious offense is non-violent, and 85 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.

Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state's department of corrections' report (2023).⁴
## Endnotes ¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Matthew Jacobs, and Edward E. Rhine, *Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Connecticut *(Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, February 2021), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/connecticut_doi_report_2_9_21.pdf.](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/connecticut_doi_report_2_9_21.pdf.)

² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.

³ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43.

⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, "National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.Connecticut Department of Correction Research Unit, Average Confined Inmate Population and Legal Status (Wethersfield, CT: Connecticut Department of Correction, 2024), accessed October 1, 2024, https://portal.ct.gov/-/media/doc/pdf/monthlystat/stat01012024.pdf, 1. Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “Prisoners - Statistical Tables,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners](https://bjs.ojp.gov/library/publications/list?series_filter=Prisoners). ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2019, the prison population decreased 34 percent, changing from 19,321 in 2010 to 12,823 in 2019. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity In 2019, 43 percent of people in prison were Black, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex In 2019, 93 percent of people in prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age In 2019, 33 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types In 2019, 47 percent of people in prison were in prison for violent offenses and 53 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people in prison were incarcerated for public order (27 percent) offenses. ```{=html}
```
## Sentence Lengths In 2019, 30 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 2 and 4.9 years. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2012 to 2019, the number of people released from prison decreased 37 percent, dropping from 12,836 in 2012 to 8,093 in 2019. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` In 2019, 9 percent of parole-eligible people released in Connecticut were released past their parole eligibility year, which is an increase of 19 percent from 2012. ```{=html}
```
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2019, 50 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases. ```{=html}
```
## Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} ### Race and Ethnicity In 2019, 40 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` ### Sex In 2019, 87 percent of people released from prison were male. ```{=html}
``` ### Age In 2019, 35 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
## Offense Types In 2019, 19 percent of people released from prison were in prison for violent offenses and 81 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people released from prison were incarcerated for public order (55 percent) offenses. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Disparities ## Disparities in Parole Release ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs-disp} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: ```{=html}
No significant disparities in incarceration rates past parole eligibility were identified between Black and White individuals, as indicated by a Relative Rate Index (RRI) of 1.

``` ```{=html} No significant disparities in incarceration rates past parole eligibility were identified between Hispanic and White individuals, as indicated by a Relative Rate Index (RRI) of 1.
``` ### Sex ```{=html}
``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_male_connecticut.png){width=1177} ```{=html} In 2019, Male were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.7 times higher than females, when accounting for prison population sizes in Connecticut.
``` :::

The chart below shows the average time served in prison by offense type and sex in 2019. The largest disparity was observed among robbery offenses, where males spent on average 3 more years in prison compared to females.

``` ::: ::: {.cell} :::
## Notes on Disparities **Understanding Disparities in Parole Outcomes** In examining disparities in parole release rates, this analysis uses the Relative Rate Index (RRI) as a metric to assess differences in incarceration rates past parole eligibility across race, ethnicity, and sex. The RRI is calculated by dividing the rate for Black or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals, with values above 1.0 indicating that a group experiences the outcome at a higher rate relative to White individuals, and values below 1.0 indicating a lower rate. This allows for a comparative analysis of which groups are more likely to remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility. **Metrics Displayed in Visualizations** Our visualizations present three main metrics to highlight disparities across race, ethnicity, and sex: - **RRI of Parole Release:** This metric compares the proportion of individuals past parole eligibility within the total prison population by race, ethnicity, and sex. It provides a snapshot of who remains in prison despite being eligible for parole. - **Average Time Past Parole Eligibility:** For those individuals who remain incarcerated past their parole eligibility date, we show the average additional time spent in prison. This is broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex, highlighting the length of delay in parole release among different groups. - **Average Total Time Served:** We also display the average total time served in prison by race, ethnicity, and sex, providing insight into sentencing and incarceration length disparities. **Data Considerations** Our analysis focuses on Black, Hispanic, and White categories to align with data limitations from the National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP), which categorizes individuals as White, Black, Hispanic, or Other. The term “Hispanic” is used here to align with NCRP terminology. Data collection and reporting practices vary significantly across states: - Race and Ethnicity Identification: Some states collect race and ethnicity data based on intake officers’ assessments, while others allow individuals to self-identify. This may result in variability and influence RRI calculations. - Combined vs. Separate Categories: Some states combine race and ethnicity into a single category, while others separate them. This inconsistency can impact the accuracy and comparability of data, sometimes leading to an underreporting of Hispanic individuals and mixed-race individuals or an overrepresentation of non-Hispanic White individuals. - Including 'Other' Category in Analysis: In states where populations categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" are substantial, we include these groups in the analysis. **Small Sample Sizes and Interpretation** Interpret findings with small numbers cautiously, as they may introduce variability and yield less reliable estimates. We exclude individuals categorized as "Other race(s), non-Hispanic" when the sample size is too small to make meaningful comparisons. According to NCRP, the “Other race(s)” category may include American Indian or Alaska Native, Asian, Native Hawaiian or Other Pacific Islander, and individuals identifying as more than one race. Small sample sizes can produce unreliable or potentially misleading results in disparities analysis. ::: --- title: "Connecticut" ---
